nomination_explanation.explanation_of_why_you_are_nominating_th:
I am nominating Rev. Angela Monique Carter for the MLK Difference Maker Award because for more than fifteen years she has embodied Dr. King’s vision of a beloved community through persistent, concrete work in South Sacramento. Her leadership in service and advocacy, her deep commitment to mentoring youth and emerging leaders, and her creative, collaborative approach to addressing systemic inequities have transformed lives and built bridges across racial, generational, and denominational lines.

Rev. Carter is the founding director of Bridge Builders Sacramento, a small but highly active nonprofit based in the Meadowview neighborhood. Through this organization, she has led year‑round efforts to address educational disparities, food insecurity, and community violence. For example, when the pandemic hit in 2020, she quickly coordinated a network of churches, neighborhood associations, and local businesses to create a weekly drive‑through food distribution at a local church parking lot. At its peak, that effort provided fresh produce and staples to more than 300 families per week, many of whom were Black, Latino, or immigrant families that had lost work.

Her advocacy extends beyond direct service. Angela has become a respected voice at Sacramento City Council meetings and school board hearings, where she consistently lifts up the concerns of parents and young people who often feel unseen. She helped organize a coalition that successfully pushed the district to expand restorative justice programs in three South Sacramento high schools, reducing suspensions and creating more supportive environments for students of color. Several students she mentored through that process are now in college and still call her for guidance.

Mentoring and coaching are at the heart of her work. Every Wednesday evening, Angela hosts a small group for high school and college‑age youth, many of whom are the first in their families to consider higher education. She helps them with college applications, scholarship essays, and job readiness skills, but she also talks honestly about race, faith, and identity. One young man, Marcus, who lost his older brother to gun violence, has shared publicly that Angela’s steady presence—showing up at his games, checking on his grades, and helping him process his grief—kept him from dropping out of school. He is now in his second year at Sacramento State, studying social work.

Angela is also an innovator. Recognizing that many families in her neighborhood were wary of traditional institutions, she helped pilot a “Porch Conversations” model: small, outdoor gatherings hosted at residents’ homes where neighbors could talk about public safety, mental health, and educational concerns in a non‑threatening, culturally rooted way. Those conversations led directly to a multilingual resource guide, co‑created with youth, that connects families with local counseling services, legal aid, and after‑school programs. Rather than waiting for people to come to her, she consistently goes to them.

While she is an ordained minister, Angela’s work is intentionally inclusive and interfaith. She frequently partners with Black, Latino, and Asian‑American congregations, as well as secular community groups, to host voter education forums, back‑to‑school supply drives, and MLK Day of Service projects. Last year, she coordinated a joint service day that brought together over 200 volunteers from eight different churches and community organizations to repaint a local elementary school, build raised garden beds, and create a mural designed by students honoring Dr. King and local civil rights leaders.

What stands out most about Rev. Carter is her consistency and humility. She does not seek the spotlight; instead, she amplifies the voices of youth, parents, and elders in the neighborhood. She is often the first to arrive and the last to leave, whether it is a community clean‑up, a late‑night hospital visit, or a city hall hearing that runs long. Her work is not a one‑time project; it is a sustained, deeply relational presence that has earned her the trust of people across lines of race, class, age, and faith.

In a time when many feel discouraged by division and injustice, Rev. Angela Monique Carter offers a living example of Dr. King’s call to "a tough mind and a tender heart." Through practical service, courageous advocacy, and creative mentoring, she has made a measurable difference in Sacramento and inspired a new generation to believe that change is possible. For these reasons, I believe she is profoundly deserving of the MLK Difference Maker Award.
